items.title: Asks patients about biggest concerns at the beginning of the visit
Reviews learning points and goals from the previous visit
Assesses patient's knowledge from last visits before providing new concepts
Uses different educational materials available at the sites (handouts, flip charts, pictures) to explain concepts
Prioritizes the specific counselling/teaching objectives, preferably using SMART criteria, that are needed in each visit
Involves the patient in decision making (changing regimens, learning goals, ...)
Uses the teach-back or return demonstration to ensure the patient understands what has been taught
Uses empathic language avoiding judgmental language and fear tactics
Finishes the conversation with encouraging comments for achieving goals, such as confidence statement
Performs a comprehensive initial evaluation of a patient with diabetes including history, physical exam, identification of complications and baseline investigations
Identifies what patients are type 1, type 2 and other types based on clinical presentation and revises the diagnosis when new information emerges
Measures blood glucose at each visit and interprets pre- and post-prandial levels of glucose according to recommended targets

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Calculates the initial total daily dose of insulin using available insulin regimens

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Assesses glycemic control using glucose logbooks, clinical history, available laboratory results (HbA1c) and adjusts treatment safely and adequately

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Safely titrates insulin doses for food insecurity

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Safely titrates insulin doses for sick day

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Safely titrates insulin for Ramadan

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Safely titrates insulin doses for physical activity

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Safely transitions patients between insulin regimens when indicated

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 1 DM
items.title: Initiates the use of correction scales and teaches them appropriately

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 2 DM
items.title: Initiates metformin correctly including correct dosing and contraindications

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 2 DM
items.title: Titrates correctly metformin to effective dose

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 2 DM
items.title: Adds second line therapy (sulphonylurea) when glycemic targets are not achieved

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 2 DM
items.title: Starts insulin therapy when glycemic targets are not achieved

items.category: TREATMENT ADJUSTMENT AND INDIVIDUALIZATION — TYPE 2 DM
items.title: Titrates insulin therapy in type 2 if uncontrolled glucose targets or HbA1c
Diagnoses and initiates correct management or referral for symptomatic hypoglycemia
Prioritizes prevention and correction of hypoglycemia when adjusting diabetes treatment
Understands and orders ketone testing when indicated
Identifies hyperglycemic emergencies and initiates first line stabilization or referral
Understands indications for hospitalization in hyperglycemic status and sends for hospitalization

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Screens for feet complications in each visit and identifies patients at risk of foot complications

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Assesses eye and refers for evaluation in the first visit and every year

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Assesses kidney damage ordering urine proteins and kidney function (if available) in the first visit and every year

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Assesses sexual dysfunction and refers to the urologist if it happens

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Starts ACE inhibitors if BP > 140/80 and/or evidence of kidney damage

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Measures creatinine in patients with ACE inhibitors after starting or increasing the dose within the next 4 weeks. Holds if there is an increase of more than 30% from the initial creatinine levels

items.category: SCREENING AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT FOR CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Adds statins to all patients with Diabetes Mellitus over 45 years old
Teaches a patient how to use a home glucometer and document blood glucose into logbook, blood sugar ranges and treatment goals
Teaches insulin administration and injection techniques, and insulin storage
Teaches on correction scales when appropriate for the patient
Teaches nutrition and diet principles
Teaches sick day management and DKA warning signs
Teaches about how to adjust insulin with physical activity
Integrates care with ANC services or Obstetrics/Gynaecology units
Programs frequent monitoring (every 2–4 weeks until week 30 and every 2 weeks until 38 weeks) and adjusts insulin regimen based on glucose logbook
Starts low dose aspirin (75–150 mg) in week 12
Identifies symptoms suggestive of heart failure
Recognizes signs of clinical decompensation and correctly assesses/categorizes volume status
Uses history to correctly classify heart failure according to NYHA functional class
Interprets basic echocardiography findings from a report and classifies correctly the underlying condition of heart failure based on that
Differentiates management between cardiomyopathies with low ejection fraction and hypertensive heart diseases (hypertensive cardiomyopathy)
Initiates heart failure mortality reducing drugs (beta blockers, ACE inhibitors) for cardiomyopathy safely based on HF protocol
Titrates heart failure mortality reducing medicines safely (beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, spironolactone) based on HF protocol
Identifies adverse effects of ACE inhibitors, beta blockers and spironolactone
Initiates diuretics safely when there are signs of HF decompensation in adults and/or children
Titrates diuretics up and down based on symptoms, physical exam and renal function
Monitors renal function & potassium levels in patients with ACE inhibitors and/or spironolactone
Recognizes heart failure decompensation and common triggers (infection, anemia, arrhythmia, medication, non-adherence) and refers appropriately for hospitalization when indicated

Consistently recognizes when a patient's condition exceeds their level of autonomy and seeks timely specialist support, providing clear clinical summaries and specific questions to guide decision-making
Performs and interprets basic ECG findings relevant to heart failure and valvular diseases
Identifies signs of heart failure in a chest X-ray
Recognizes clinical features of rheumatic heart diseases with valvular complications (murmur on examination)
Initiates appropriate medical management including secondary prophylaxis with penicillin
Initiates beta blockers in mitral stenosis and identifies the indication for rate control
Identifies atrial fibrillation on ECG and starts or indicates anticoagulation to prevent systemic embolic complications
Refers to cardiologist always regardless of the symptoms or severity of stenosis
Refers women patients with cardiac diseases to family planning and consults for planning delivery
Identifies loud murmur on physical examination
Looks for and correctly identifies cyanosis and clubbing in children as possible signs of congenital heart diseases
Initiates appropriate initial management based on suspected diagnosis and clinical status
Refers to pediatric cardiologist always for definitive evaluation
Provides counselling related to medication dose and side effects (e.g. syncope) and supports patient adherence by explaining the purpose and importance of the medication
Educates patients and caregivers to recognize early signs of cardiac decompensation and explains common triggers of heart failure decompensation
Teaches appropriate self-management strategies when indicated: including diuretic adjustment according to symptoms and/or ACE inhibitors and beta blockers titration
Provides counselling on diet and lifestyle modifications relevant to cardiac diseases such as salt reduction, physical activity
Counsels patients and families appropriately on family planning when cardiac diagnosis is present
Educates patients on dietary considerations when taking warfarin
Avoids ACE inhibitors, spironolactone and atenolol in pregnant women
Uses pregnancy-safe alternatives (hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate) if necessary and other beta blockers apart from atenolol
Uses low doses of beta blockers, ACE inhibitors and spironolactone in breastfeeding
Avoids warfarin during first and third trimester of pregnancy if anticoagulation is indicated
Coordinates care with cardiology and antenatal services for pregnant women with heart diseases
Demonstrates an understanding of basic ultrasound principles
Understands the probe care document and implements it correctly
Demonstrates knowledge about key ultrasound adjustments to optimize image quality (axis, depth and gain)
Shows correct technique to perform the 5 basic views in cardiac ultrasound (PLAX, PSAX, A4C, Subcostal, IVC) in children
Shows correct technique to perform the 5 basic views in cardiac ultrasound (PLAX, PSAX, A4C, Subcostal, IVC) in adults
Describes the echo features of cardiomyopathy, mitral stenosis, hypertensive heart diseases, right heart failure, pericardial diseases
Describes the echo features for the most common congenital heart diseases
Correctly identifies diagnosis according to the PEN-Plus protocol
Able to clearly and correctly report echo results
Proactively uses the diagnostic algorithm in the country/PEN-Plus facility for people with signs and symptoms of SCD
Accurately interprets results of SCD rapid tests and/or Hb Electrophoresis
Differentiates sickle cell trait from sickle cell diseases based on diagnostic test results and clinical context
Performs a comprehensive baseline assessment of a newly diagnosed SCD patient including history, physical examination and appropriate laboratory investigations
Initiates hydroxyurea at the correct starting dose based on patient weight and baseline laboratory evaluation (FBC/Hb)
Adjusts HU dosing appropriately according to updated patient weight and clinical response and laboratory monitoring
Monitors correctly for hydroxyurea toxicity using full blood count or isolated Hb (if FBC not available), kidney and liver function when indicated
Recognizes clinical and laboratory signs of HU toxicity
Temporarily stops hydroxyurea when toxicity occurs
Re-starts hydroxyurea correctly when toxicity is resolved
Prescribes folic acid supplementation to reduce complications related to chronic hemolysis
Assesses immunization status in children using the under-5 card and recommends indicated vaccines according to national guidelines
Uses antibiotic and/or malaria prophylaxis when indicated based on National Policy (usually for under 5)
Provides routine follow-up care including monitoring of growth, complications, treatment adherence and medication tolerance
Recommends temporarily stopping HU during the first trimester of pregnancy
Re-starts HU during the second trimester if pain crisis, need of transfusion and/or other complications in agreement with patient

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Identifies vaso-occlusive crisis and determines when hospitalization is required

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Identifies suspected acute chest syndrome and refers for hospitalization

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Recognizes signs of stroke and refers for hospitalization

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Suspects acute splenic sequestration or severe acute hemolytic anemia and refers for hospitalization

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Identifies severe anemia requiring urgent blood transfusion and triggers

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Identifies indications for non-urgent blood transfusion and applies appropriate hemoglobin thresholds

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE AND CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Identifies signs of infection/sepsis, checks for malaria, starts empiric broad spectrum antibiotics based on immunization status and refers for hospitalization when necessary
Evaluates severity of pain using appropriate tools
Identifies acute vaso-occlusive pain crisis
Initiates pain management using the WHO analgesic ladder
Prescribes morphine at age-appropriate dosing when indicated/available
Demonstrates ability to titrate up and down morphine for pain and knowledge of side effects
Identifies when acute pain management requires hospitalization. Evaluates patients for other potential causes of pain or comorbid conditions
Able to differentiate acute from chronic pain
Identifies patients with AVN as cause of chronic pain
Utilizes adjunct medication in chronic pain management and non-pharmacological approaches to pain
Refers to physical therapy if available and appropriate
Safely transitions between oral and intravenous opioid therapy during hospitalization
Manages acute chest syndrome safely including blood transfusion
Manages stroke safely including blood transfusion
Manages fever in inpatients with appropriate evaluation and antimicrobial treatment
Manages other complications such as priapism adequately
Provides counselling to parents and patient related to acute and chronic complications (especially management of pain), early warning signs and when to come to the hospital
Recognizes the role and types of psychosocial support needed in SCD management

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF SCD DURING AND BEFORE PREGNANCY
items.title: Refers patients of childbearing age for family planning as needed and discusses risks of pregnancy in SCD

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF SCD DURING AND BEFORE PREGNANCY
items.title: Re-starts HU during the second trimester if pain crisis, need of transfusion and/or other complications in agreement with patient

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF SCD DURING AND BEFORE PREGNANCY
items.title: Initiates preventive therapies during pregnancy: aspirin 150 mg after week 12 and calcium 1.5 g daily

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF SCD DURING AND BEFORE PREGNANCY
items.title: Ensures appropriate ANC follow-up schedule (every 4 weeks until week 28, every 2 weeks until week 36 and weekly after week 36)

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF SCD DURING AND BEFORE PREGNANCY
items.title: Starts low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) in inpatient pregnant women or outpatient with history of thrombosis or known clotting disorder
Performs a structured clinical assessment for asthma including symptom history (wheeze, cough, nocturnal symptoms, triggers) and physical examination
Recognizes typical patterns of asthma symptoms, including reversibility and flares
Differentiates asthma from other causes of chronic respiratory symptoms such as COPD, post-tuberculosis lung diseases, heart failure or infection
Understands and performs spirometry (if available) as a diagnostic tool and differentiates between obstruction and restriction
Assesses and classifies asthma using GINA or local guideline symptom control tools
Identifies correctly asthma as controlled, partially controlled or uncontrolled
Identifies risk factors for severe asthma exacerbation (poor adherence, frequent use of reliever, environmental exposure)
Prescribes inhaled steroid as the foundation of treatment for all patients with persistent asthma when available
Prescribes and adjusts inhaled therapies according to asthma severity and control using stepwise treatment (step up and step down)
Avoids use of LABA (long-acting beta agonist) as monotherapy in asthma
Avoids routine use of salbutamol and theophylline tablets in asthma due to low effectiveness and higher toxicity
Prescribes oral corticosteroids appropriately during acute exacerbations
Prescribes low oral doses of corticosteroids only if no other drugs available for symptomatic control out of exacerbations
Counsels patients on identifying and avoiding common asthma triggers (dust, smoke, cold air, ...)
Counsels on stopping smoking
Demonstrates inhaler technique to patient and verifies patient ability to perform it correctly (including spacer)
Teaches patients how to adjust treatment according to symptom control and how to monitor symptoms and recognize poor asthma control; confirms understanding
Recognizes signs of asthma exacerbation including breathlessness, wheezing, reduced peak flow, or inability to speak in full sentences
Identifies danger signs requiring urgent referral or hospitalization (severe respiratory distress, hypoxia, altered consciousness, ...)
Initiates appropriate treatment of mild or moderate exacerbations in outpatient settings and monitors patient response to treatment and determines when escalation of care or referral is required

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS — COPD AND POST-TUBERCULOSIS LUNG DISEASE
items.title: Evaluates patients with chronic respiratory symptoms using structured history (including smoking, TB, occupational exposures)

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS — COPD AND POST-TUBERCULOSIS LUNG DISEASE
items.title: Recognizes clinical features of COPD including chronic cough, sputum production, and progressive dyspnoea

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS — COPD AND POST-TUBERCULOSIS LUNG DISEASE
items.title: Recognizes common post-TB patterns on chest X-ray

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS — COPD AND POST-TUBERCULOSIS LUNG DISEASE
items.title: Differentiates obstructive and restrictive respiratory patterns using spirometry when available

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF COPD AND POST-LUNG DISEASES
items.title: Identifies exacerbation of COPD and when to refer for admission

items.category: MANAGEMENT OF COPD AND POST-LUNG DISEASES
items.title: Correctly manages exacerbation when there is no indication for hospitalization (bronchodilators, steroids and antibiotics when indicated)
Measures BP correctly several times using an appropriate technique before giving a diagnosis of elevated blood pressure

Performs a focused clinical evaluation to identify target organ damage with the appropriate baseline investigations: kidney function, electrolyte, blood glucose, and other tests according to guidelines
Assesses cardiovascular risk factors including diabetes, obesity, smoking and family history
Starts medication adequately (first line drugs at adequate dosage): long acting CCB, diuretics or ACEI based on hypertension stage and comorbidities
Initiates combination therapy when indicated (stage 2 or severe hypertension)
Titrates up medication when BP targets are not achieved. Adds third drug or increases doses to maximum ones based on adherence or availability if uncontrolled with medium doses of 2 drugs
Optimizes treatment regimen before labeling hypertension as resistant
Initiates spironolactone as fourth drug for resistant hypertension (unless hyperkalaemia)
Uses beta-blockers as additional therapy when needed for resistant hypertension or when indicated by comorbid conditions
Maintains antihypertensive therapy when blood pressure targets are achieved unless adverse effects occur
Checks for kidney function before and after starting and adjusting ACEI if available
Considers stopping or consulting on the continuation of ACEI if creatinine levels increase > 30% after initiation from the baseline
Identifies important adverse effects of drugs (e.g. angioedema and cough with ACE inhibitors, oedema with amlodipine)
Can differentiate between hypertensive urgency and emergency
Recognizes clinical signs of acute organ damage (stroke, acute heart failure, chest pain, kidney failure, altered mental status) requiring admission
Manages hypertensive urgency correctly at OPD with oral medications and close follow-up
Appropriately manages hypertensive emergency
Provides counselling on lifestyle interventions including salt reduction, weight control, smoking cessation and physical activity
Educates patients about hypertension as a chronic condition requiring long-term treatment and monitoring, and the importance of medication adherence
Identifies when a patient has chronic kidney failure based on lab markers (creatinine and glomerular filtration)
Performs essential first-visit evaluations for patients with CKD including blood pressure, urine dipstick, HIV, kidney function, electrolytes and abdominal USS (if available)
Identifies the common causes of CKD in the local context: HTN, DM, obstructive uropathy, HIV-associated kidney failure, others
Checks for symptoms and signs of urine retention in male patients > 50 years or with symptoms and signs of benign prostatic hypertrophy
Treats BP with a goal of < 130/80 mmHg, starting with low-dose ACE inhibitor if proteinuria and any other antihypertensive medication if proteinuria is absent
Checks creatinine increases after starting ACE inhibitors and adjusts when the increase is > 30% from the baseline or potassium levels are > 5.5 mEq/L after consulting an expert
Avoids the use of ACE inhibitors in stage 4 or 5 unless prescribed by a nephrologist after clinical evaluation
Identifies and avoids nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs, gentamycin, tenofovir and other drugs toxic to kidney)
Manages volume overload using furosemide and/or thiazide in any of the stages
Screens and treats anaemia associated with CKD with iron supplementation if Hb < 10 g/dl
Identifies the main complications of CKD (hyperkalaemia, volume overload, encephalopathy) and refers for admission and urgent dialysis when needed
Monitors progression of CKD through clinical assessment and laboratory data
Refers all patients with CKD for nephrology evaluation when specialized care is available
Refers for family planning to women in childbearing age
Identifies patients with advanced CKD who might benefit from palliative care when dialysis and transplant is not feasible

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS OF NEPHROTIC SYNDROME
items.title: Identifies signs and symptoms of nephrotic syndrome (generalized oedema, foamy urine, periorbital swelling, ...)

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS OF NEPHROTIC SYNDROME
items.title: Identifies common causes of nephrotic syndrome by age group (minimal change disease, post-infectious, HIV-associated, ...)

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS OF NEPHROTIC SYNDROME
items.title: Uses urine dipstick testing to detect significant proteinuria as an initial diagnostic tool

items.category: ASSESSMENT AND DIAGNOSIS OF NEPHROTIC SYNDROME
items.title: Differentiates nephrotic syndrome from other causes of oedema such as heart failure or liver disease
Initiates corticosteroid therapy at appropriate dosing for suspected steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome
Gradually tapers down corticosteroids when remission is achieved
Identifies steroid resistance when proteinuria does not improve after adequate course of therapy and refers to specialist
Initiates ACE inhibitors as adjunct therapy to reduce proteinuria when appropriate
Manages oedema using diuretics (furosemide and spironolactone)
Adds statins (if available) when indicated (age ≥ 8 years, persistent proteinuria and high cholesterol)
Conducts a structured seizure history (onset, duration, frequency, triggers, pattern, ...)
Screens for potential precipitants of crisis if needed: sleep deprivation, missed doses, alcohol, ...
Assesses adherence, side effects and barriers to treatment if needed (cost, distance, superstition, stigma, ...)
Classifies seizures into generalized vs focal based on clinical history
Differentiates epileptic seizures from common mimics such as syncope, febrile seizures, psychogenic non-epileptic events
Performs a focused neurological examination to identify focal neurological deficits or other signs suggesting secondary causes
Recognizes when to refer for the performance of secondary tests such as CT or EEG
Identifies when to refer to higher level care: recurrent seizures despite 2 optimized drugs, suspicion of structural cause (focal neurological signs or persistent headache)
Initiates appropriate first line antiseizure medications available locally or based on local protocol at adequate doses
Follows stepwise protocol for drug selection. Uses a max dose of one drug before switching to another
Titrates dose of anticonvulsant according to level of control and side effects
Changes drugs in case there is intolerance or lack of response
Considers interactions with anticonvulsants and other drugs (tuberculosis treatment, antiretroviral treatment or contraceptives)
Addresses stigma and misconceptions about epilepsy in the community and family
Counsels patients on adherence to medication and avoidance of known seizure triggers and to keep a seizure diary
Provides counselling to women of childbearing age regarding pregnancy planning and epilepsy management
Avoids sodium valproate in women of childbearing age when other alternatives are available
Provides counselling about contraception and refers to the contraception units when necessary
Identifies palliative care needs of clients and their family/caregiver
Provides standard pain assessment (application of concept of TOTAL Pain) and assessment of other symptoms
Uses standard pain management (using WHO analgesic ladder)
Understands the use of adjuvants for the management of pain and secondary effects
Knows how to apply symptom control for common symptoms (including non-pharmacological approaches)
Knows the side effects and drug-drug interactions of common symptom control medication (top priority: side effects of opioids and how to prevent them)
Limits the use of drugs that are not indicated in a palliative care situation
Assesses psychosocial needs, provides social support, and refers to a mental health specialist
Knows how to provide or when and where to refer for spiritual and cultural care
Understands the special considerations needed for paediatric clients
Limits the use of drugs that are not indicated in a palliative care situation
Plans for end-of-life care when indicated
Links the patient to any home-based care or community resources
Knows and uses non-invasive scores to diagnose liver cirrhosis (APRI, USS) with adequate interpretation of abdominal USS findings
Identifies aetiologies of liver disease (e.g. viral hepatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, steatosis) in clinical history and complementary tests
Performs targeted physical exam looking for: jaundice, ascites, pedal oedema, spider naevi and asterixis to identify complications
Identifies signs of volume overload decompensation (ascites)
Identifies signs of liver encephalopathy and its triggers
Identifies signs of GI bleeding and performs rectal examination when suspected
Initiates specific treatment for underlying treatable conditions such as hepatitis B and C
Initiates diuretics correctly when ascites or pedal oedema
Performs paracentesis when indicated (ascites refractory to diuretics)
Titrates diuretics correctly up and down with electrolyte and kidney function monitoring
Treats correctly initial signs of liver encephalopathy when there is no indication for admission with lactulose, and identifies triggers
Identifies when to refer for admission when the patient shows signs of decompensation
Gives beta blockers for patients with previous oesophageal varices bleeding
Titrates beta blockers correctly to achieve benefit (lowering HR to at least 55 bpm)
Gives antibiotics for secondary prophylaxis of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
